sentences.text: Now, the work-- the work I did was any-- any other laborer or any other person who worked in a factory on the-- on the floor level.
And I didn't feel-- I didn't feel that that was done unjustly by because why wouldn't I work when everybody else had to work?
As far as I-- as far as my father and my uncles were concerned, they were, of course, doing their work what they always did-- done before.
And no different whatsoever.
So that part-- the work part was no-- no problem to me.

The living conditions were-- were, unfortunately, more of a problem because, as I said before, a family was just four, or-- or two, or 16-- [INAUDIBLE]-- it doesn't matter how many children-- they were all in one room.
Now, but you have families who were there and a family of seven or eight people, they were still in the one room.
Everybody-- every family was allocated one room, regardless And their-- if a house-- a normal house has three rooms, there was three families living in it.
There was one-- was one kitchen and one bathroom.
It was really a matter of luck whether you were ever in a house with-- with two large families, or three large families, or smaller families.
These were the conditions.
And-- and I think we were very happy, even under those conditions, to-- to wait to the end of the war where we were there, instead of being marched off and sent to-- sent to Auschwitz.
Yes.
Yes, they worked out-- they worked out sort of a common-- common eatery because they had to.
They had to.
The people-- 20, 25 people in a house.
There had to be an arrangement.
To-- otherwise, it would've been fights, or-- or arguments, or whatever.
And nobody would have been better off.
Yeah.
Yes, there was medical supply.
Yes, there were Jewish doctors.
There was no-- there was no-- I don't think there was any trouble in there.
Yes, yeah, yeah.
The time-- the time spent in the ghetto was very short.
So it would have been-- it would have taken a longer time, a longer spent.
I don't know what it would have been.
But the whole ghetto was-- was set up in-- in April.
And the the whole ghetto was liquidated in July.
So it was a very, very temporary ghetto.
Well, what happened was is I-- I think I've-- we go over again the same-- the same event.
But everybody was marched out from the ghetto-- group by group-- 100, 200, 300 people at a time through-- through town and into the-- into the brick factory in Budakalasz.
And as I said before, describe what-- what-- what it was like-- that walk from-- from one point to the other point.
So once we arrived in the-- in the brick factory, I think that was very-- the message got home.
Because there was no-- no such a thing as a bed.
There was no such a thing.
We-- we had-- we had-- I think we stayed together as a family.
The family was-- consisted of-- if I remember well-- close to maybe 17 people.
And we stayed together in the same area.
And we had straw.
Straw.
Yes, there was a roof.
Yeah.
There was a roof.
And I-- if you've ever been to a brick factory, there are certain-- certain places, certain sections where you can-- open areas.
And there are sections which has a roof.
We-- we had a roof over our head.
We had nothing else but the roof over our head.
And-- and the floor, the concrete floor, was-- was straw in it.
And that was-- that was everybody's bed.
Oh, the-- the sanitary was-- that is-- that is what I think were the first degradation.
So that's when we really know what-- what degradation meant, because the-- the-- the sanitary arrangements were latrines.
They were dug into the-- into the-- into the earth.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was just one latrine for women and one latrine for men.
And that was, I think, other people has lost-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] dignity-- has-- have lost their dignity and felt that we are not exactly being treated as VIPs.
And people begin-- had begun to understand that we were in serious trouble.
Because they-- the treatment what we received there, the way the food was received there-- there was nobody had any food there anymore for-- left what they took-- what they had before.
Because whatever there was, a little was consumed in the ghetto.
And when you have one suitcase to carry, I don't think that many people would just think of carrying food.
The others carried their own little belongings what they-- what they have to be able to change clothes.
We were also warned where when we left the ghetto that we'll be walking.
We knew where Budakalasz was.
So no, everybody knew that nobody could carry a huge, big suitcase for 10 kilometers or more.
It's impossible.
So really, we went-- we-- we traveled light.
Sorry?
The authority, I think, the-- the police and-- and the Hungarian authorities.
Well, that actually was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- the speech you were given.
Really not in the brick factory, but when we were leaving the ghetto.

Before we were marched out of the ghetto, one of the high-ranking gendarmes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got-- got us together and made a little speech of how happy he is to witness the-- the cleansing of-- of Hungary from Jews.
And his parting words were, you are being taken away from here never to be returned ever again.
So we were-- that was a part-- that was a-- the-- one for the road, you know?
For-- that was what-- what-- what we were told.
So there was-- we didn't need to be told anything else because only-- the only thing we saw in the-- in the brick factory that day by day, the train pulled up.
And people were loaded up.
And when you've got 75 people in-- in one-- one wagon, one of those cattle trucks-- They counted 75, whether it was family or not family.
You tried to stay together.
Every family tried to say together.
It was 75.
It was cut off.
And it doesn't matter who it was, what-- what the relationship was.
[INAUDIBLE] to the next truck.
But nobody needed any explanation, you know.
No, our family wasn't separated.
We managed to-- to stay together.
And we were clinging to each other.
We all managed to get into the same-- same wagon, same cattle truck.
But-- and I remember it was in one corner, there was bread piled up for the journey.
And in the other corner, there were-- there were the buckets for people to use.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think I-- I-- I wouldn't be-- I wouldn't be quite sure how many people died by the time we got to Auschwitz, which-- which took five days.
But I know that there were quite a number of people who-- who didn't arrive to Auschwitz.
Not that it would have made any difference.
But it made a difference to the-- to the family, knowing that their father or their mother were-- was lying next to them in the wagon dead.
Of course, it might had been.
But by that time, people-- people lost feeling for-- you know, everybody was so [INAUDIBLE] inclined.
Everybody was just think of his own, very own life, very own survival, and their-- and their children's survival.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was a terrible situation.
Parents who were respected by the children, grandparents, they-- they were helpless.
All-- all deprived of any sort of authority, which a parent can exercise over a child.
It was simply on animal level.
Absolutely animal level.
People who-- who were very private and very-- very introverted, they-- they had to go-- they had to-- they go to the toilet among 75 other people.

I mean-- I mean, I don't think there's any need to describe the-- the-- the feeling of people, of-- or how they must have felt-- how-- how a person, a grandmother must have felt when-- when-- when the daughter or the grandchild had to help her with-- with her everyday business, which she wouldn't have dreamt of even talking about, never mind doing it in public.
Or-- so I think the last bit of-- of dignity was just-- was just stripped away.
The train stopped-- the train stopped the first time-- from the time it started, it stopped in a place called in Hungarian, Kasha, and in Czech, Kosice.
And it was practically the border between-- between Poland-- not far from the border anyway.
And every-- every-- every wagon, every cattle truck had-- had one person responsible.
And this person was let out.
He had to report the number of-- of dead.
They have taken off the dead people.
And they gave us water.
They gave us water.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I remember that our-- our-- the person in our truck was my professor from-- from the Hungarian gymnasium.
And being-- being-- being what he was used to, being-- being somebody who always had the authority and being respected, he was silly enough to ask the authorities for more facilities.
And-- and he asked for medication because some of the older people were dying.
They were not dead, but dying.
So all I can say is that he would have-- he couldn't have done favors for himself because they-- they brought him back beaten up absolutely to-- the man-- I don't think the man recovered.
He-- he was in bad shape.
Do you remember that professor's name?
Yeah.
I remember.
Fisher.
His name was Fisher, Professor Fisher.
Yeah.
His name was [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Arnold.
Arnold Fischer.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yeah, we arrived sometimes in the morning hours.
I don't know.
We had no watch.
I don't know what time it was.
But we were-- they opened up the-- the trucks.
And the first orders, we had-- beside to getting out, families to stay together.
The families to stay together.

So I always think it was a very clever move because families, when they stay together, it meant a ray of hope that-- when families stay together, it means that there might be life for those who were still alive after all.
The reason why they asked families to stay together was because they wanted to avoid any sort of panic and any sort of unnecessary [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] delay by people going hysteric.
And-- and especially for the children, to stay with their mothers.
They didn't-- they didn't care about, really, who was young and who was old as long as they had a sort of an order.
The-- the Germans are well know for order.
And this was they wanted order.
The second command was to leave all their belongings.
Put the belongings-- make sure that their belongings are marked, that our names are on it, and leave it where it is.
And we will get it-- will get it back.
If our name is properly marked, we will get it back after we-- we've been allocated to our quarters.
It was the second command.
And then, of course, we started with five abreast.
And we started to walk.
We started to march.
We-- we got to the-- to the end of the road.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mothers who were holding the children and elderly people, they were sent to the right.
And people who were-- who were independent and who looked strong enough to-- to work, useful, they were sent to the left.
We didn't know what it was.
We-- we had no idea because even after we were sent to the left, and people to the right, there was another section where women were separated from men.
So first-- the first selection came-- first selection came when the-- the useful from the-- from the liabilities, sort of.
See, they never-- they never tried to separate the mother from the child.
I have not seen-- I watched it for quite a few minutes, maybe 10 minutes, the people in front of me.
And I have not seen anybody tearing children away from each other.
I don't know.
Maybe other people, it was different.
But it went in complete order.
Absolute, complete order.
There could have been somebody handed a child over to a younger person, to an older person.
But nobody was forced to do anything.

So-- after the first-- so after we were sent to the-- to the left, men and women, and we saw-- saw young ones with their mothers and the-- and the elderly go to the right, we still didn't know what was happening.
We just thought, this is quite normal.

Because people-- we didn't-- we-- we didn't like it because it was the first-- the first realization that families stayed together wasn't quite-- quite so 100% acceptable because all of a sudden, families got torn apart.
But there was nothing we could do.
It was happening.
Everything was happening very, very quickly.
And then came that we went to the left, my father and myself, and within one or two minutes, there was another table with another group of officers there where men were separated from-- from women.
And there was no question about it.
The men were separated from women.
And men went one-- one way, and women went-- went the other way.
From then on, we just marched into our area, which was called Birkenau.
Birkenau, as-- as I'm sure you know, it was the transit-- transit camp of Auschwitz.
And it didn't take very long afterwards.
Within-- within a matter of minutes as we arrived, the people who were there already, whether they were there a week, or a day, or-- or not much longer, nobody stayed very-- very long in Birkenau.
They went-- they left Birkenau one way or the other.
So they-- then they told us what was happening.
Sorry?
No, the other-- the other prisoners.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, how did we react?
Some people reacted because we were already, I think, more or less-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I said-- I don't think people felt as they were people anymore.
I think people who felt that they were still people--
So I know that-- that quite a few people ran against the barbed wire, and they got electrocuted.
I know that.
I was seeing two or three myself.
They couldn't-- they couldn't take it.
And when people are talking about their families, guessing-- Some people have a sick-- sick humor.
And some people said, I wonder whose child is going out in the chimney now.
And not everybody could take it.
Some people-- some people prayed.
And some people just-- as I said before, they-- the-- the will of survival, to survive, for whatever reason a person wanted to survive, I think that was the strongest feeling everybody.
Anybody who gave up, they didn't survive.
I don't know.
Everywhere-- ?] I think at this stage, there was no such a thing as common prayer.
First of all, nobody would have been game enough because you were not allowed to-- to congregate.
Everybody who was-- who was praying, what they were sort of praying, I don't know.
I-- I can't tell you that.
Because at that stage, I had my father with me.
And we were together.
And-- and we-- we sort of gave strength to each other.
I was 15 at the time.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not-- really not many 15-year-olds came through Auschwitz.
Because-- for-- I don't know what the reason was.
Probably the reason was because I was about as tall as I am now.
I was 15.
And call it a miracle.
Call it good luck.
I don't know what it is.
But we managed to get through.
And-- and we stayed together until-- until my father died.
But after-- after being-- being in Auschwitz, they were putting so much-- what-- what do you call this-- drugs into the food.
Not Valium, but--
Pardon?
Yeah, I think something or other.
I forgot the name was it.
Everybody was a tragic.
Everybody was-- you know, people were not-- people are not the same people as-- as they would be otherwise.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were-- were-- apart from-- from really half animal, half man.
People were-- were much calmer.
There simply was-- everybody accepted that there is no way out of here.
There's nothing we can do about it.
You have to accept it and have to try to survive.
Regardless what-- what other people say, regardless what we-- what we saw sometimes-- women with shorn hair, which we saw a couple times.
We got a glimpse of women in the-- in the other camp, which is not close.
But as we were going for food and to-- to get food, sometimes you could get a glimpse of-- of in the distance of women.
And at least we had the hope that our women were still alive-- the women who-- up to a certain age.
It didn't come to that.
It didn't come to sickness.
Anybody who couldn't stand up for two or three hours on a zahlappell in the morning, in the freezing cold, or the heat, or whatever, it-- they didn't have a chance to become sick.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They was very, very quickly taken away.
Yes, I did receive once-- once news that they were together.
My mother and my sister were together get in-- in the women's camp.
Yeah.
I don't know anymore who told me, but this sort of news came through.
When you-- this is why we all-- all tried to get to of the kitchen because the kitchen was the place where all this sort of news somehow got through.
And to work in the kitchen, it wasn't exactly-- exactly anybody's dream to work in the kitchen because it-- it-- it was-- it was sort of a calculated risk.
